h1Title: Flat Feet (Pes Planus): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Flat feet (pes planus) is a condition in which the medial longitudinal arch of the foot is absent or reduced, causing the entire plantar surface to contact the ground during standing. The medial arch normally rises 15-18 mm above the floor in adults; in pes planus it flattens below a clinically significant threshold. Flat feet affect approximately 26% of adults and up to 45% of children under age 6, making them one of the most prevalent lower-limb variants encountered in podiatric practice. Most cases in children are flexible and physiological, resolving spontaneously by age 6-8. In adults, symptomatic flat feet cause medial foot and ankle pain, excessive pronation, and secondary overuse injuries up the kinetic chain to the knee, hip, and lumbar spine. Treatment ranges from supportive footwear and custom orthotics to reconstruction surgery for rigid or progressive deformity.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 26% of adults have pes planus (Dunn et al. 2004; Shibuya et al. 2010); up to 54% in studies using strict radiographic criteria
keyStats.demographics: Children aged 3-6 have physiological flat feet in up to 45% of cases; adult flat feet are more prevalent in men, in individuals with obesity, and in people over 60
keyStats.avgAge: Physiological flat feet in childhood typically resolve by age 6-8; adult-acquired flat foot from posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) peaks at age 40-60
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 170-200 million adults worldwide have clinically apparent flat feet based on global prevalence extrapolation

definition: Pes planus (ICD-10: M21.4) is a structural or postural reduction in the height of the medial longitudinal arch resulting in hindfoot valgus (eversion) and forefoot abduction. The condition is classified into flexible (mobile) and rigid (fixed) subtypes, which differ in management and prognosis. In flexible flat foot, the arch reconstitutes on tip-toe standing and in non-weight-bearing; the subtalar and midtarsal joints retain full passive mobility. In rigid flat foot, the arch remains absent regardless of weight-bearing status, and joint mobility is reduced or absent — commonly caused by tarsal coalition, posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD), inflammatory arthritis, or neurological conditions. Flexible flat feet are further subdivided by the Johnson-Strom classification for adult-acquired flat foot: stage I represents tenosynovitis of the tibialis posterior without deformity; stage II shows a passively correctable flatfoot deformity; stage III is a rigid flatfoot; and stage IV involves deltoid ligament failure and tibiotalar tilting. Pes planus is managed by podiatrists and orthopedic foot and ankle surgeons, with most cases responding to conservative intervention. Acquired adult flat foot secondary to tibialis posterior tendon rupture requires early recognition and intervention to prevent irreversible deformity.

typesClassification.description: The most common type. Arch is visible in non-weight-bearing but collapses on standing. The subtalar joint is mobile. Found in nearly all infants and most children; arch development completes by age 6-10. Rarely symptomatic in children; in adults may cause fatigue, medial arch pain, and pronation-related overuse injuries.

typesClassification.type: Flexible flat foot with a tight Achilles tendon
typesClassification.description: Heel cord contracture combined with flatfoot forces the foot into increased pronation to achieve heel contact. Requires directed calf-stretching and, if refractory, gastrocnemius recession.

typesClassification.type: Adult-acquired flat foot (posterior tibial tendon dysfunction)
typesClassification.description: Degeneration or rupture of the tibialis posterior tendon, the primary dynamic supporter of the medial arch. Produces a progressive, often asymmetrical flatfoot deformity in middle-aged adults. Staged Johnson-Strom classification I-IV guides treatment from conservative (stage I) to complex reconstruction (stage III) to arthrodesis (stage IV).

The arch remains collapsed in all positions due to tarsal coalition (bony/fibrous/cartilaginous bar between hindfoot bones), advanced posterior tibial tendon failure, inflammatory arthritis, or neuropathic (Charcot) joint. Requires surgical correction for symptomatic cases.

Rare congenital rigid flatfoot with a rocker-bottom appearance caused by dorsal dislocation of the navicular on the talus. Detected at birth; treated with serial casting (reverse Ponseti technique) and surgical correction if needed.

primarySymptoms: Medial foot and ankle pain, typically along the tibialis posterior tendon from the navicular tuberosity to the medial malleolus, worsening with prolonged standing or walking.
Generalized foot fatigue and aching after activity, often localized to the arch and inner heel, relieved by rest and elevation.
Visible flattening of the medial longitudinal arch when standing, with the entire plantar surface contacting the ground.
Hindfoot valgus deformity — the heel visibly rolls inward when viewed from behind, and the Achilles tendon appears bowed medially ('too many toes sign').
Excessive wear on the medial heel and inner forefoot edge of footwear from hyperpronation during the gait cycle.
Heel pain (plantar fasciitis) from increased tensile load on the plantar fascia secondary to arch collapse.
Shin splints and medial tibial stress syndrome from increased tibialis posterior loading during running and prolonged walking.
Knee pain from patellofemoral syndrome or medial compartment strain, caused by tibial internal rotation and knee valgus from hyperpronation.
Lower-back pain from altered pelvic tilt and lumbar lordosis compensating for lower-limb malalignment.
earlyWarningSigns: Unilateral collapse of one arch in an adult who previously had bilateral arches — the asymmetry suggests tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction rather than constitutional flatfoot.
Increasing shoe medial edge wear on only one side, a practical early indicator of unilateral pronation increase.
Medial ankle swelling and tenderness along the tibialis posterior tendon course in a middle-aged woman, representing tenosynovitis before tendon failure.
Difficulty walking on tip-toe on the affected side — loss of heel rise on single-leg calf raise is a sensitive sign of tibialis posterior tendon insufficiency.
emergencySigns: Acute medial ankle pain with a palpable snap or gap in the tibialis posterior tendon, followed by sudden and complete arch collapse — requires urgent imaging and surgical consultation.
Acute bilateral flat feet with diffuse joint swelling and erythema suggesting new-onset inflammatory arthritis (rheumatoid arthritis, reactive arthritis) — requires systemic evaluation.
Flatfoot with insensate foot, warm skin, and bony collapse (Charcot neuroarthropathy) in a patient with diabetes or peripheral neuropathy — requires urgent offloading and endocrinology co-management.

causes.description: Infants are born with a fat pad that fills the medial arch and normally resorbs as intrinsic foot muscles and ligaments strengthen during weight-bearing development. Arch formation continues until approximately age 6-10; flat feet before this age are physiological and require no intervention unless symptomatic.

causes.cause: Posterior tibial tendon degeneration or rupture
causes.description: The tibialis posterior is the primary dynamic supporter of the medial arch. Tendinopathy from chronic overloading or rupture — most common in middle-aged women with obesity, hypertension, or corticosteroid use — produces the most common adult-acquired flatfoot. Stage I-II PTTD accounts for the majority of symptomatic adult flat feet referred to specialist care.

Lax spring ligament, deltoid, and plantar fascial attachments allow the medial column to sag under body weight. Generalized hypermobility (Beighton score ≥4) is present in a significant minority of flat-foot patients and predicts poorer response to conservative treatment.

Abnormal bony, fibrous, or cartilaginous bridge between hindfoot bones (most commonly calcaneonavicular or talocalcaneal) restricts subtalar motion and causes a painful rigid flatfoot in adolescents, typically presenting at age 8-16 when the coalition ossifies.

Rheumatoid and psoriatic arthritis destroy the spring ligament and talonavicular joint capsule, producing a characteristic hindfoot valgus flat-foot pattern. Flat foot in rheumatoid disease is almost always bilateral and progressive.

Each kilogram of body weight generates 3-4 times ground reaction force at peak mid-stance. Obesity (BMI ≥30) is independently associated with pes planus in adults — prevalence is nearly twice that of normal-weight adults — through combined mechanical overloading and reduced neuromuscular arch support.

riskFactors.description: Obesity nearly doubles the risk of symptomatic flat feet and accelerates posterior tibial tendon degeneration. Weight loss of 5-10% body weight meaningfully reduces medial arch loading.
Adult-acquired flat foot secondary to PTTD is 3-4× more common in women than men, attributed to differences in tendon biology, hormonal effects on tendon collagen, and footwear.

Tibialis posterior tendon tensile strength decreases with age; ligament laxity increases. Prevalence of symptomatic adult flat feet rises sharply after age 40 and continues increasing through the sixth decade.

Systemic or peritendinous corticosteroid use weakens tendon collagen and is a recognized risk factor for tibialis posterior tendon rupture. Intratendinous steroid injection is avoided in PTTD management.

Peripheral neuropathy from diabetes disrupts the proprioceptive feedback that maintains dynamic arch support. Charcot neuroarthropathy produces a catastrophic rigid flat foot in poorly controlled diabetes.
First-degree relative with flat feet increases personal risk approximately 2× through inherited foot morphology including a round talar dome, lax spring ligament, and short Achilles tendon.

Workers standing on hard floors for more than 6 hours per day have significantly higher rates of symptomatic flat feet and PTTD compared with sedentary occupations. Anti-fatigue matting and footwear with arch support reduce occupational risk.

affectedDemographics: Physiological flat feet are nearly universal in infants and present in 45% of children aged 3-6, declining to approximately 15% by age 10 as arch development completes.
Adult symptomatic flat feet predominantly affect women aged 40-60 in the context of PTTD; men are more commonly affected by tarsal coalition presenting in adolescence.
Individuals with obesity (BMI ≥30) have up to 2× the prevalence of flat feet compared with normal-weight adults (Shibuya et al. 2010).

Military recruits with flat feet have higher rates of stress fractures and lower-extremity overuse injury (up to 2× in some cohort studies), though screening programs for exclusion have been largely abandoned as most flat feet are asymptomatic.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical and, where indicated, radiographic. The clinician takes a detailed history of onset (childhood vs adult), symptom laterality (bilateral physiological vs unilateral PTTD), activities that worsen pain, and systemic disease. Physical examination with the patient standing evaluates arch height (Navicular Drop Test — drop of more than 10 mm is significant), hindfoot valgus angle, and the 'too many toes' sign (more than two toes visible lateral to the heel on posterior inspection). The single-leg heel-rise test assesses tibialis posterior tendon function: inability to rise on one leg or heel valgus failing to convert to varus on heel rise indicates PTTD. Subtalar joint range of motion distinguishes flexible from rigid flat foot. The Jack's test (passive dorsiflexion of the hallux) reconstitutes the arch in flexible flat foot via the windlass mechanism — failure to reconstitute suggests structural deformity or tendon failure. Weight-bearing foot radiographs (AP, lateral, oblique, hindfoot alignment view) quantify Meary's angle (talus-first-metatarsal angle, normally 0°; negative angle indicates pes planus), calcaneal pitch (normally 18-20°), and talonavicular coverage angle. MRI of the ankle and hindfoot is obtained when PTTD is suspected to grade tendon signal and tears. CT is valuable for tarsal coalition characterization.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies medial arch collapse during weight-bearing; navicular drop of more than 10 mm indicates clinically significant pronation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician marks the navicular tuberosity and measures its height from the floor in sitting and standing. Painless; takes 2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses tibialis posterior tendon function — inability to rise or failure of heel varus conversion indicates PTTD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient stands on one foot and rises onto tip-toe. May cause mild medial ankle pain in PTTD. Result noted immediately. Test repeated 10 times to assess endurance.

diagnosticTests.test: Weight-bearing foot radiographs (AP, lateral, hindfoot alignment)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures Meary's angle, calcaneal pitch, and talonavicular coverage to quantify deformity and guide surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three X-ray views taken while standing; takes about 5 minutes. Painless. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades tibialis posterior tendon signal, longitudinal splits, and partial or complete tears; differentiates PTTD stages
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan lying still, no radiation. Gadolinium contrast may be used. Results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes type, extent, and location of tarsal coalition when suspected in an adolescent with rigid flat feet
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Short 5-minute scan with radiation. Patient lies still in scanner. Results within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps medial forefoot over-loading pattern and quantifies pronation for orthotic prescription and outcomes monitoring
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Walk barefoot or with in-shoe sensors across a pressure plate; takes 10 minutes and is painless.

treatmentOverview: Management is guided by flexibility, symptoms, and underlying cause. Asymptomatic flat feet in children or adults require no treatment. Symptomatic flexible flat feet in children are managed with activity modification, supportive footwear, and custom foot orthotics; the evidence does not support routine orthotic prescription in asymptomatic children. In adults, the first-line approach for flexible flat feet and early PTTD (stages I-II) consists of arch-supportive footwear, custom orthotics with medial heel post and arch fill, and physiotherapy targeting tibialis posterior and intrinsic foot-muscle strengthening. A University of California Biomechanics Laboratory (UCBL) orthosis or Arizona brace provides more aggressive hindfoot control for stage IIB deformity with forefoot abduction. Calf-stretching corrects associated equinus, which amplifies pronation forces. NSAIDs and immobilization in a below-knee walking boot are used during PTTD tenosynovitis flares. Corticosteroid injection is avoided around the tibialis posterior tendon due to rupture risk. When conservative treatment fails after 3-6 months, or when deformity is rigid (stages III-IV PTTD or tarsal coalition), surgery is indicated. Surgical options range from tarsal coalition resection and flexor hallucis longus tendon transfer for early PTTD to medializing calcaneal osteotomy and lateral column lengthening for moderate deformity, through to triple arthrodesis for rigid end-stage disease. Congenital vertical talus is managed with serial casting (reverse Ponseti technique) and limited surgical correction. All surgical patients require postoperative orthotic management and physiotherapy to maintain correction.

medicalTreatments.name: Custom foot orthotics with medial heel post and arch support
medicalTreatments.description: Cast or scanned custom insoles control hindfoot valgus and support the medial arch. UCBL orthosis or Arizona brace used for severe or flexible deformity requiring aggressive medial control. Must be worn in all footwear throughout the day.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces medial arch loading and pain by 30-40% in RCT evidence; clinically meaningful pain reduction in stage I-II PTTD in prospective cohort studies

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive footwear with stability/motion-control construction
medicalTreatments.description: Shoes with a medial post, firm midsole, and straight last reduce excessive pronation during gait. Motion-control running shoes are appropriate for active patients; extra-depth clinical footwear for those with comorbid neuropathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined footwear and orthotic therapy superior to either alone; stability footwear alone reduces overpronation by 15-20% compared with neutral shoes

medicalTreatments.name: Below-knee walking boot immobilization
medicalTreatments.description: A total-contact or CAM (controlled-ankle-motion) boot is used for 6-8 weeks in acute PTTD tenosynovitis or early tendon failure to reduce tendon loading and allow inflammation resolution before rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces acute PTTD pain and swelling in 70-80% of patients; combined with physiotherapy produces better outcomes than immobilization alone

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy: tibialis posterior strengthening and calf stretching
medicalTreatments.description: Eccentric and concentric exercises for the tibialis posterior tendon (resisted foot inversion, single-leg calf raise progression) combined with Achilles/gastrocnemius stretching. Core and hip-abductor strengthening addresses kinetic chain contributors.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tibialis posterior strengthening programs improve pain and function scores by 30-50% at 3-month follow-up in PTTD stages I-II; calf stretching reduces equinus-driven pronation

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily; naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course oral anti-inflammatory medication during acute tenosynovitis flares of PTTD. Not a long-term management strategy for chronic flat feet.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces acute inflammatory pain within 7-14 days; no evidence for long-term benefit beyond symptom management

surgicalOptions.name: Tarsal coalition resection (calcaneonavicular or talocalcaneal)
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of the abnormal bony or fibrous bar between tarsal bones, restoring subtalar motion in adolescents with painful rigid flat foot from coalition. Fat or muscle graft is interposed to prevent re-bridging.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good to excellent results in 70-90% for calcaneonavicular coalition; talocalcaneal coalition has a lower success rate of 50-70% due to residual subtalar stiffness

surgicalOptions.name: Medializing calcaneal osteotomy with flexor digitorum longus (FDL) tendon transfer
surgicalOptions.description: Translates the heel medially to correct hindfoot valgus; FDL transfer augments the degenerated tibialis posterior. Standard procedure for stage IIA adult-acquired flat foot.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good-to-excellent outcomes in 80-90% at 2 years; AOFAS hindfoot scores improve by 35-45 points on average

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral column lengthening (Evans osteotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Bone graft at the anterior calcaneus lengthens the lateral column, reducing talonavicular uncovering and forefoot abduction. Used for stage IIB deformity with significant forefoot abduction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant reduction in talonavicular uncoverage; patient satisfaction over 80% at 2 years when combined with medializing calcaneal osteotomy

surgicalOptions.name: Triple arthrodesis (subtalar, talonavicular, calcaneocuboid fusion)
surgicalOptions.description: Fuses the three hindfoot joints in corrected alignment. Reserved for rigid stage III-IV PTTD or end-stage inflammatory arthritis with fixed deformity. Sacrifices hindfoot motion permanently but provides lasting pain relief.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Union rate above 90%; patient satisfaction 75-85% at 5 years; adjacent joint arthritis develops in 20-30% at 10+ years

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) for tibialis posterior tenosynovitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Injection of autologous PRP into the tibialis posterior tendon sheath in stage I PTTD. Small studies show reduction in tenosynovitis pain at 3-6 months compared with corticosteroid, with a better safety profile. Corticosteroid is avoided due to rupture risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT) for plantar fasciitis co-management
alternativeTreatments.description: ESWT delivered to the plantar fascia insertion in flat-foot patients with concurrent plantar fasciitis. Meta-analyses support meaningful pain reduction at 3 months. Complements orthotic treatment rather than replacing it.

whySeeSpecialist: A podiatrist or foot and ankle surgeon should evaluate flat feet when medial foot or ankle pain limits daily activities, when deformity is progressive or unilateral in an adult, or when a child has pain, toe-walking, or arch collapse persisting beyond age 8. Early specialist assessment of PTTD is critical — stage I-II responds well to conservative treatment, while stage III-IV may require complex reconstruction.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a podiatrist or orthopedic foot and ankle surgeon who regularly manages PTTD and hindfoot reconstruction. Confirm access to dynamic pedobarography for orthotic prescription, MRI for tendon assessment, and an experienced orthotist for UCBL and Arizona brace fabrication.
hospitalCriteria: Foot and ankle surgical unit with hindfoot alignment radiography capability
Gait laboratory or pedobarography for objective pronation quantification
Orthotist service for custom UCBL brace and foot orthotic fabrication
Pediatric orthopedic expertise for tarsal coalition and congenital cases
keyFacilities: Podiatric and orthopedic foot and ankle clinics
Multidisciplinary gait and foot deformity centers
preventionStrategies: Wear supportive footwear with arch support and a motion-control or stability category construction from adolescence onward, particularly during high-load activities.
Maintain healthy body weight to reduce medial arch loading; each kilogram of weight loss reduces mid-stance arch force by 3-4 kg.
Perform regular intrinsic foot-muscle and tibialis posterior strengthening exercises (short-foot exercise, single-leg calf raises) to maintain dynamic arch support.
Stretch the calf muscles and Achilles tendon daily to prevent equinus contracture, which amplifies pronation forces.
Avoid peritendinous corticosteroid injection into the tibialis posterior tendon; if tenosynovitis is present, use boot immobilization and physiotherapy instead.
lifestyleModifications: Wear prescribed custom orthotics in all footwear throughout the day, including when walking at home.
Perform daily tibialis posterior eccentric strengthening exercises as prescribed by the physiotherapist.
Use a below-knee walking boot during acute PTTD flares to allow tendon recovery.
Avoid barefoot walking on hard floors, which provides no arch support and increases tibialis posterior strain.
Reduce activity impact during symptomatic periods — switch to low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling) when running or standing for prolonged periods worsens pain.
Monitor footwear wear pattern monthly and replace shoes when the medial heel shows excessive breakdown.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) to reduce tendon and joint inflammation in PTTD
Adequate protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to support tendon collagen repair during rehabilitation
Calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-1,000 IU/day) for bone health, particularly relevant in surgical patients
Colorful fruits and vegetables providing vitamin C and polyphenols that support collagen synthesis
avoid: Excess sodium, contributing to lower-leg edema that worsens arch loading
Alcohol, which impairs tendon healing and increases fall risk in elderly patients with flat feet
High-calorie processed foods that contribute to obesity, the leading modifiable risk factor for adult flat feet

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact exercise (cycling, swimming, water walking) is appropriate for symptomatic flat feet. Running on hard surfaces worsens PTTD symptoms; trail running or grass running with orthotics is better tolerated. Single-leg calf raises progressed from bilateral to single-leg over 6-8 weeks strengthen tibialis posterior without excessive tendon loading. Return to high-impact sport after surgical reconstruction typically requires 3-6 months for osteotomy and 6-12 months after arthrodesis.
dailyManagement: Insert orthotics in footwear every morning before weight-bearing activities.
Complete the prescribed tibialis posterior and intrinsic foot-strengthening routine, typically 15-20 minutes.
Perform gastrocnemius-soleus stretching (30 seconds, 3 repetitions each side) morning and evening.
Inspect feet for signs of skin breakdown or blistering under orthotics, especially in patients with diabetes.
Apply ice to the medial ankle for 15-20 minutes after prolonged activity if tenosynovitis pain is present.
Record daily pain levels and functional limitation to share with the podiatrist at review appointments.
prognosis: Physiological flat feet in children resolve in 95% of cases by age 8-10 without treatment. Adult asymptomatic flat feet do not require intervention and do not inevitably become symptomatic. For symptomatic flexible flat feet and stage I-II PTTD managed with orthotics and physiotherapy, 70-80% of patients achieve satisfactory function without surgery. Stage IIA-IIB disease progressing despite conservative treatment shows good-to-excellent results after surgical correction in 80-90% of cases at 2-year follow-up. Stage III rigid flat foot requires triple arthrodesis, which provides durable pain relief in 75-85% of patients but sacrifices hindfoot motion permanently. Late complications after triple arthrodesis include adjacent-joint osteoarthritis in 20-30% at 10 years, requiring further management. Without treatment, progressive PTTD leads to rigid deformity requiring increasingly complex surgery; early recognition and conservative management are the most important prognostic factors.
recoveryTimeline: Boot immobilization for PTTD tenosynovitis: 6-8 weeks to symptom resolution. Orthotic fitting and physiotherapy: functional improvement over 3-6 months. Post-surgical recovery: osteotomy procedures allow protected weight-bearing at 6-8 weeks and full activity at 4-6 months; triple arthrodesis requires 10-12 weeks non-weight-bearing and 12-18 months for return to full activity.
complications: Progression from flexible to rigid flat foot if PTTD is untreated — once the hindfoot becomes fixed, conservative options are ineffective and reconstruction surgery is complex.
Plantar fasciitis from increased tensile stress on the plantar fascia secondary to arch collapse; detected by medial heel pain on first steps in the morning.
Stress fractures of the second and third metatarsals from altered load distribution; identified by localized metatarsal tenderness and MRI bone edema signal.
Ankle osteoarthritis in stage IV PTTD from chronic deltoid ligament failure and tibiotalar tilting — requires ankle arthrodesis or replacement.
Charcot neuroarthropathy in diabetic patients with neuropathy — rapid bony collapse requires urgent offloading to prevent limb-threatening deformity.
Knee and hip osteoarthritis from lifelong hyperpronation and internal tibial rotation — association is epidemiological rather than causally proven but supports early arch correction.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association — Flatfoot
supportResources.description: Patient guide on flat foot types, orthotics, and when to seek podiatric care.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society — Adult Flatfoot
supportResources.description: Orthopaedic surgeon perspective on PTTD staging and surgical reconstruction options.

supportResources.name: NHS UK — Flat Feet
supportResources.description: NHS patient information covering causes, when treatment is needed, and self-care advice.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes sharp inferior heel pain on first steps in the morning that improves with activity. Flat feet is a structural deformity causing medial arch and ankle pain throughout activity. Flat feet are a risk factor for plantar fasciitis; both conditions frequently coexist and require separate management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tarsal tunnel syndrome causes burning, tingling, or numbness along the plantar foot from tibial nerve compression at the medial ankle. Flat feet cause aching, fatigue-type arch pain without neurological symptoms. Nerve conduction studies confirm tarsal tunnel syndrome; flat feet are diagnosed clinically and radiographically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achilles tendinitis causes posterior heel and tendon pain above the calcaneal insertion that is worst in the morning and after exercise. Flat feet cause medial arch and tibialis posterior pain. Both conditions involve hindfoot biomechanics; tight calf muscles contribute to both.

relatedConditions.name: Hallux Valgus (Bunion)
relatedConditions.relevance: Pes planus is a primary biomechanical driver of hallux valgus progression; flat feet and bunions coexist in up to 50% of cases.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Arch collapse increases tension on the plantar fascia; plantar fasciitis is the most common secondary condition in patients with flat feet.

relatedConditions.name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Hindfoot valgus from flat feet can stretch or entrap the tibial nerve at the tarsal tunnel, causing secondary neuropathy.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Hyperpronation from flat feet places abnormal stress on the Achilles tendon, increasing tendinopathy risk.

relatedConditions.name: Charcot Foot
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe neuropathic flat foot collapse (Charcot neuroarthropathy) is a complication of uncontrolled diabetes and shares clinical features with advanced pes planus.

faqs.question: Are flat feet a medical problem?
faqs.answer: Flat feet are a medical problem only when they cause symptoms or deformity. The majority of people with flat feet have no pain and need no treatment. Symptomatic flat feet — causing medial arch pain, fatigue, ankle pain, or contributing to other lower-limb problems — benefit from orthotics and physiotherapy.

faqs.question: Can flat feet be corrected without surgery?
faqs.answer: Flexible flat feet can be managed effectively without surgery in most adults through custom orthotics, supportive footwear, and tibialis posterior strengthening exercises. Surgery is reserved for cases where conservative treatment fails after 3-6 months, when deformity is rigid, or when the posterior tibial tendon has ruptured and arch collapse is progressive.

faqs.question: Do children grow out of flat feet?
faqs.answer: Yes, in the majority of cases. Up to 45% of children aged 3-6 have physiological flat feet, and the arch develops as intrinsic foot muscles and ligaments mature. By age 10, flat feet resolve in 95% of children. Persistent flat feet after age 10, particularly if rigid or symptomatic, warrant podiatric evaluation.

faqs.question: What causes flat feet in adults?
faqs.answer: Adult flat feet are most commonly caused by degeneration of the tibialis posterior tendon (posterior tibial tendon dysfunction), which is the primary arch-supporting structure. Other causes include ligamentous laxity, obesity, inflammatory arthritis, tarsal coalition, and diabetes-related Charcot neuroarthropathy.

faqs.question: Are orthotics useful for flat feet?
faqs.answer: Custom foot orthotics with medial arch support and heel posting reduce pronation, redistribute plantar load, and improve pain in symptomatic flat feet. They are most effective when combined with supportive footwear and physiotherapy. They do not correct bony deformity but manage symptoms effectively in the majority of flexible flat-foot cases.

faqs.question: Can flat feet cause knee pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Excessive pronation from flat feet causes tibial internal rotation and knee valgus, loading the medial knee compartment and patellofemoral joint. Studies link pes planus to patellofemoral syndrome and medial compartment knee osteoarthritis. Correcting foot pronation with orthotics can reduce knee pain by 20-30% in affected patients.

faqs.question: What exercises help flat feet?
faqs.answer: Tibialis posterior strengthening (resisted foot inversion, single-leg calf raise) and intrinsic foot exercises (short-foot exercise, toe-spread and hold, towel scrunches) are the most evidence-based. Gastrocnemius-soleus stretching reduces equinus that worsens pronation. Daily execution of 15-20 minutes is needed for clinical benefit.

faqs.question: What shoes are best for flat feet?
faqs.answer: Motion-control or stability-category footwear with a straight last, firm medial post, and stiff midsole best support flat feet. Avoid flexible, neutral-cushion, or minimalist shoes. Extra-width shoes accommodate orthotic insoles. Footwear should be replaced when the medial heel shows more than 5 mm of compression wear.

faqs.question: How is flat foot diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Flat foot is diagnosed clinically by observing arch collapse on standing, performing the navicular drop test and single-leg heel-rise test, and assessing subtalar joint motion. Weight-bearing foot X-rays confirm deformity severity using Meary's angle and calcaneal pitch. MRI is obtained when posterior tibial tendon dysfunction is suspected.

faqs.question: Can flat feet cause back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, indirectly. Hyperpronation from flat feet causes tibial internal rotation, pelvic tilt, and altered lumbar lordosis, which can produce low-back pain. Studies show correction of flat feet with orthotics improves back pain scores in a proportion of affected patients, although direct causation is difficult to establish.

faqs.question: When should flat feet be treated in children?
faqs.answer: Asymptomatic flat feet in children under age 8 require no treatment. Intervention is indicated if the child has pain, fatigue, toe-walking, difficulty keeping up with peers, or if the arch fails to develop by age 8-10. A podiatric evaluation with weight-bearing X-rays guides management in symptomatic cases.

faqs.question: What is posterior tibial tendon dysfunction?
faqs.answer: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) is degeneration or tearing of the tibialis posterior tendon, the main arch-supporting structure in adults. It causes a progressive, often unilateral flat foot deformity in middle-aged adults, particularly women. Classified stage I-IV: early stages respond to orthotics and physiotherapy; advanced stages require reconstructive surgery.

faqs.question: Is flat foot surgery painful?
faqs.answer: Surgery is performed under regional or general anesthesia, so the procedure is painless. Postoperative pain is managed with elevation and oral analgesics for 2-3 weeks. Calcaneal osteotomy and tendon transfer procedures typically cause moderate postoperative discomfort lasting 2-4 weeks. Triple arthrodesis has a longer recovery but the majority of patients report significant pain improvement once healed.

faqs.question: Can flat feet get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Physiological flat feet in children typically improve with age. Adult flat feet may worsen progressively, particularly when driven by posterior tibial tendon dysfunction — with each stage requiring more complex treatment. Obesity, prolonged standing, and ignoring early symptoms are the main factors accelerating progression.

faqs.question: Do flat feet disqualify you from sports or the military?
faqs.answer: Asymptomatic flat feet are no longer a universal disqualifier from military service or competitive sport. Evidence shows most flat-footed military recruits perform comparably to those with normal arches, though they have modestly higher rates of stress fractures. Symptomatic flat feet requiring activity modification are assessed on a case-by-case basis.

metaTitle: Flat Feet (Pes Planus): Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Flat feet (pes planus): absent medial arch causing foot, ankle, and knee pain. Symptoms, causes, orthotics, surgery options and recovery reviewed here.
